it: In un cristallo il volume di un atomo è quasi tutto vuoto; il nucleo sta al centro.
en: In a crystal the volume of an atom is almost entirely empty; the nucleus is at the centre.

it: Radioattività e radiazioni α.
en: Radioactivity and α radiation.

it: Descrizione dell'apparato sperimentale.
en: Description of the experimental apparatus.

it: Esecuzione dell'esperimento e risultati sperimentali.
en: Carrying out the experiment and experimental results.

it: Interpretazione dei risultati.
en: Interpretation of the results.

it: Equazione di Schrödinger in tre dimensioni e flusso di probabilità.
en: The Schrödinger equation in three dimensions and probability flux.

it: Formula di diffusione di Rutherford.
en: Rutherford's scattering formula.

it: Confronto tra teoria ed esperienza.
en: Comparison between theory and experiment.

it: Appendice 1. Equazione di Helmholtz.
en: Appendix 1. The Helmholtz equation.

it: Appendice 2.
en: Appendix 2.

it: Altre immagini del capitolo
en: Other images in this chapter

it: Schema dell'apparato: sorgente, collimatore, lamina d'oro, rilevatore.
en: Diagram of the apparatus: source, collimator, gold foil, detector.

it: Il preparato radioattivo Am241.
en: The Am241 radioactive source.

it: La lamina d'oro con le fenditure di collimazione.
en: The gold foil with the collimating slits.

it: Il detector al silicio e l'amplificatore di misura.
en: The silicon detector and the measuring amplifier.

it: Caduta della resistenza del detector al passaggio di una particella α.
en: Drop in the detector's resistance as an α particle passes.

it: L'amplificatore dal lato dei morsetti di uscita.
en: The amplifier from the side of the output terminals.

it: L'impulso squadrato e la tensione di soglia U.
en: The squared pulse and the threshold voltage U.

it: L'intero apparato sperimentale.
en: The entire experimental apparatus.

it: La camera aperta con tutti i componenti.
en: The chamber open with all its components.

it: La camera chiusa con il goniometro.
en: The chamber closed, with the goniometer.

it: Urto contro atomi immaginati come sferette piene.
en: Collision with atoms imagined as solid little spheres.

it: Con nuclei piccolissimi la lamina è quasi trasparente ai raggi α.
en: With tiny nuclei the foil is almost transparent to α rays.

it: Geometria della diffusione.
en: Geometry of the scattering.

it: Onda piana incidente
en: Incident plane wave → plane wave plus diverging spherical wave.

it: Un fascio stretto: solo l'onda sferica raggiunge il detector.
en: A narrow beam: only the spherical wave reaches the detector.

it: Angolo solido del rivelatore.
en: Solid angle of the detector.

it: Nella parte finale della scheda mostreremo un metodo
en: In the final part of the card we will show a method that will let us determine the positive charge contained in the nucleus; in this way we will also indirectly measure the number of electrons belonging to the atom.

it: Prima di descrivere l'esperimento ci conviene spendere
en: Before describing the experiment it is worth spending a few lines to introduce the phenomena of radioactivity and to describe the α particles. In the future we will devote a card to radioactivity, so for now we want to clarify only the bare minimum needed to understand the experiment.

it: Alcuni materiali emettono spontaneamente vari tipi
en: Some materials spontaneously emit various types of high-energy particles, without being stimulated in any way from outside. These materials are called radioactive, and the beams of emitted particles are called radiations.

it: Le radiazioni possono essere osservate con vari tipi
en: The radiations can be observed with various types of detectors, more or less complicated, which we will describe in the card on radioactivity. The most sophisticated detectors also allow the type of particles to be distinguished and their physical characteristics to be determined.

it: Le particelle che si usano nell'esperimento di Rutherford
en: The particles used in Rutherford's experiment are called α particles; they have a mass practically equal to that of the helium atom and an electric charge equal to twice that of the electron. In essence, an α particle is the nucleus of a helium atom.

it: La velocità di emissione delle particelle α dipende
en: The emission speed of the α particles depends on the radioactive material used, and can be measured with deflection experiments in electric and magnetic fields similar to those described in the third card.

it: La figura 2 mostra uno schema dell'apparato.
en: Figure {0} shows a diagram of the apparatus.

it: Abbiamo un preparato radioattivo chiamato Am241
en: We have a radioactive source called Am241 that emits α particles. The beam is collimated by a slit, after which it strikes a thin gold foil 2 μm thick; the particles cross the foil and are scattered. Finally we have a detector that can be placed at various angles ϑ and that measures the distribution of the scattered particles.

it: L'esperimento viene eseguito sotto vuoto (pressione
en: The experiment is carried out under vacuum (pressure <1 mBar = 100 Pa) to allow the α particles to travel their path without colliding with the molecules of the air.

it: La figura 3 è una foto del preparato radioattivo
en: Figure {0} is a photograph of the radioactive source; the Am241 is placed on the head of a metal support and is kept in a glass container for safety reasons.

it: La figura 4 mostra la lamina d'oro dello spessore
en: Figure {0} shows the gold foil, 2 μm thick, mounted on a plastic support; two collimating slits are also visible — the one behind on the left is 1 mm wide, while the other in front on the right is 5 mm.

it: Il sistema di rilevazione è formato da un detector
en: The detection system consists of a silicon detector connected to a measuring amplifier; the photograph in figure {0} shows the detector and the amplifier.

it: Il detector è formato da un bipolo di silicio
en: The detector consists of a silicon two-terminal device with its own electrical resistance; when the sensitive surface is struck by an α particle with sufficient energy, the electrical resistance drops for an instant and then returns to its original value (fig. {0}). The mechanism of this phenomenon will be described in detail in a card devoted to semiconductor materials, so for now we do not worry about it. The measuring amplifier “senses” the change in resistance and amplifies the signal, generating an amplified pulse with the same shape as the one received at the input. The photograph in figure {1} shows the amplifier from the side of the output terminals: from the left terminal one can take the amplified pulse with its original shape, while from the right terminal one obtains a squared pulse built as figure {2} shows; the level of the voltage U can be adjusted with a knob located on the upper part of the amplifier. The squared pulse is fed to a digital counter which, by counting the number of pulses, counts the number of α particles that reach the detector. Figure {3} shows a photograph of the whole experimental apparatus: the cylinder on the left is the chamber containing the radioactive source, the gold foil and the detection sensor. The chamber is connected to a reciprocating pump that produces the vacuum; moreover, from the chamber runs the cable connecting the sensor to the amplifier shown in the centre, which in turn is connected to the digital counter seen on the right. The amplifier is powered by a 10 V DC power supply seen in the centre behind the amplifier.

it: Nella foto di figura 10 si vede la camera aperta
en: In the photograph in figure {0} the chamber is seen open with all its components — the radioactive source, the sensor and the gold foil already mounted on its support; figure {1} shows the chamber closed.

it: Nello schema iniziale (fig. 2) abbiamo visto
en: In the initial diagram (fig. {0}) we saw that the radioactive source and the gold foil were fixed while the detector could rotate; in our system it is the opposite: the detector is fixed to the wall of the cylinder, while the gold foil and the radioactive source are mounted on a rotating support — obviously the two solutions are equivalent. In figure {1} the knob on the right serves to rotate a support that is not used in our experiment, while the knob in the centre is the one that lets us rotate the support of the radioactive source and the gold foil.

it: Prima di tutto si deve aprire il cilindro
en: First of all the cylinder must be opened and the various elements arranged — the radioactive source, the slit, the gold foil and the detector. One can choose the 1 mm slit or the 5 mm one: in the first case more precise measurements are obtained, in the second faster measurements.

it: Se il cilindro è sotto vuoto bisogna prima far entrare
en: If the cylinder is under vacuum, air must first be let in by opening the dedicated valve, and only then can the lid be removed. If the gold foil is already mounted inside the cylinder, care must be taken not to let the air in too abruptly, otherwise the foil could tear because of the violent pressure variations.

it: Dopo aver disposto tutti gli elementi si chiude il cilindro
en: After arranging all the elements, the cylinder is closed and the pump is switched on for about five minutes; meanwhile the detector is connected to the amplifier and the amplifier to the digital counter, and the amplifier is powered with a 10 V DC generator.

it: Dopo aver spento la pompa si accendono il contatore
en: After switching off the pump, the counter and the amplifier are turned on, and the threshold voltage U (fig. {0}) is set to about 0.5 V with the dedicated knob on the amplifier.

it: A questo punto si possono eseguire le misure.
en: At this point the measurements can be taken. The detection angle ϑ is set with the knob on the cylinder's cap, the counter is zeroed, and the counter and a pocket stopwatch are started at the same time. After a few minutes, when a sufficient number of pulses has been counted, the counter and the stopwatch are stopped at the same time; in a table one records the angle ϑ, the number of pulses counted and the detection time. It is advisable to count at least about twenty pulses to reduce the statistical error.

it: La tabella qui sotto mostra una serie di misure
en: The table below shows a series of measurements taken with the 1 mm slit; in the fourth column the number of pulses per unit time {0} is reported. Figure 12 plots {1} as a function of ϑ; one observes that the curve is shifted to the left by about 1.2°, which simply means that the direction of the beam was not perfectly aligned with the zero of the goniometer.

it: Sapendo che la lamina d'oro è spessa 2 μm
en: Knowing that the gold foil is 2 μm thick and that a gold atom has a diameter of about 1 Å = 10⁻⁴ μm, we can calculate that the foil is about twenty thousand atoms thick. Moreover we know that a gold atom is about fifty times heavier than an α particle.

it: In base a queste informazioni se supponiamo che gli atomi
en: On the basis of this information, if we suppose that atoms are like solid little spheres and try to imagine the collision between an α particle and the gold foil (Fig. {0})

it: non riusciamo a capire come sia possibile che la particella
en: we cannot understand how it is possible for the particle to cross the foil. Under these conditions the radiation should be blocked, and yet experimentally it has been observed that almost all the particles cross the wall with a rather small deflection (<10°).

it: I risultati sperimentali si possono spiegare pensando
en: The experimental results can be explained by thinking that the atom is structured more or less like a small planetary system, with a very heavy and very small nucleus at the centre, and with a set of electrons moving in an orbital space similar to a cloud. In the next card we will study such a system with the Schrödinger equation and show how it is possible for it to remain in equilibrium. For now we pause to observe how this hypothesis agrees with the experimental results.

it: Prima di tutto, in base al modello atomico che abbiamo
en: First of all, on the basis of the atomic model we have hypothesised, we can understand why the gold foil is so transparent to α rays (fig. {0}); indeed, since the nuclear dimensions are very small, even if there are twenty thousand nuclei in a row, it is very improbable that a head-on collision occurs.

it: Nell'ultimo paragrafo calcoleremo la distribuzione
en: In the last section we will calculate the probability distribution for the scattering angle ϑ, using the Schrödinger equation and the planetary atomic model, and we will show that it agrees with our experimental results; first, however, we must generalise the Schrödinger equation to the three-dimensional case and analyse some interesting developments of a theoretical nature.

it: L'equazione di Schrödinger in tre dimensioni può essere ottenuta
en: The Schrödinger equation in three dimensions can be obtained by retracing exactly the same steps we followed to obtain the one-dimensional one, with the only nuisance of having to use a somewhat heavier formalism. The result is formally analogous:

it: La funzione
en: The function {0} is a vector in “{1} dimensions” that depends on time. We have in fact three indices corresponding to three observable quantities, that is, the three coordinates that identify the position of the particle. So one can say that the function {2} is the distribution of probability amplitudes for the triple of random variables {3}; the dependence on time indicates that the distribution can vary with time. If we want to calculate the probability distribution we must take the squared moduli:

it: Ad esempio se consideriamo un volume Ω e vogliamo
en: For example, if we consider a volume Ω and want to determine the probability that the particle is found in the volume Ω, then we must compute the integral:

it: In generale quando abbiamo una distribuzione di una certa
en: In general, when we have a distribution of a certain physical quantity — for example mass or charge — we also have the flux-density vector of that same quantity, for example the mass-flux-density vector or the charge-flux-density vector. In our case we have a probability distribution, and we expect that a probability-flux-density vector can be defined such that a continuity equation holds, similar to the one valid for any other physical quantity:

it: Cioè la diminuzione della probabilità contenuta in Ω
en: That is, the decrease of the probability contained in Ω equals the outgoing flux of the probability-flux-density vector {0} through the surface {1} that bounds the volume Ω.

it: Come è noto questa equazione può essere scritta anche
en: As is well known, this equation can also be written in differential form:

en: To better understand the analytical meaning of this formula, let us try to express the complex function {0} in terms of modulus and phase: {1}. Substituting into the formula for {2} we have:

en: {0}The underlined term simplifies because it is purely real

en: So we can write

it: Quest'ultima formula ci mostra che, il vettore densità di flusso
en: This last formula shows us that the probability-flux-density vector at a given point is proportional to the probability density at that point and to the gradient of the phase {0} of the distribution of probability amplitudes.

it: Consideriamo una particella α che incide contro una lamina
en: Consider an α particle that strikes a gold foil, and suppose we want to determine the probability distribution {0} that the particle is deflected through an angle ϑ.

it: Prima di tutto osserviamo che non ci potranno essere fenomeni
en: First of all we observe that there can be no diffraction phenomena, because the wavelength associated with an α particle is much smaller than the interatomic distance of the crystal lattice of gold; indeed:

it: sostituendo i valori
en: substituting the values {0}, {1} and {2} (the speed of the α rays will be measured in the card on radiations)

it: Per impostare il problema di diffusione con l'equazione
en: To set up the scattering problem with the Schrödinger equation we would have to specify the initial “wave packet”, that is, the initial distribution of probability amplitudes {0}, and moreover we would have to specify the electric potential {1} present inside the gold foil.

it: Osserviamo che già a questo punto abbiamo fatto una notevole
en: We note that already at this point we have made a considerable simplification; indeed the gold foil is a complex system made of an aggregate of gold nuclei and electrons. Despite this simplification the problem remains very complicated and we must find a strategy to get around it.

it: Sperimentalmente abbiamo visto che quasi tutte le particelle subiscono piccole
en: Experimentally we saw that almost all the particles undergo small deflections (say, less than 15°), while only a small percentage undergoes larger deflections. This means that it is very improbable to have a “close collision”, that is, it is very improbable that the wave packet passes close enough to a nucleus to be deflected by an angle greater than 15°.

it: Possiamo concludere che, se è improbabile che ci sia un urto
en: We can conclude that, if it is improbable to have a collision with a deflection angle greater than 15°, then it is even more improbable that there are two consecutive collisions both with an angle greater than 15°. So we can assume that the particles deflected at large angles undergo a single collision while crossing the gold foil. More correctly, we can say that they undergo a single collision of significant magnitude, plus many small collisions with zero average effect.

it: In base alla precedente ipotesi noi studieremo la diffusione
en: On the basis of the previous hypothesis we will study the scattering due to a single atom, and we will compare the theoretical and experimental results only for large angles.

it: Per impostare il problema ci rimane da definire il pacchetto
en: To set up the problem it remains to define the initial wave packet. The simplest condition is to assume that a plane wave of the type {1} comes from the direction {0}. After determining the solution in this particular case, we can use the linearity of the Schrödinger equation and build other solutions by superposing those already found. In particular, by suitably choosing the superposition, we can obtain the solution for any initial packet; indeed Fourier's theorem guarantees that any complex function can be obtained as a superposition of functions of the type {2}, {3} and {4}.

it: Ora passiamo a fare i conti:
en: Now let us do the calculations:

On the basis of these considerations we can approximate the term {3}. For the denominator we can write {4}; while for the argument of the exponential we can write {5} (fig. {6}).

en: To compute the integral we must choose a coordinate system; the most sensible choice is a polar coordinate system {0}, with the axis in the direction {1}. Indeed, in this way we have {2}, and since {3} we can write the integral in the form:

it: L'integrando non contiene la variabile β
en: The integrand does not contain the variable β, so the integration with respect to β is trivial and gives a term 2π. The integration with respect to α is done by substitution, setting {0}; in this way we have:

it: A questo punto per completare il calcolo dell'integrale
en: At this point, to complete the calculation of the integral, we must introduce the potential {0}. We could insert {1}, where {2} is the charge of the nucleus, but in this way we obtain an “oscillating” integral that cannot be computed; this problem arises because we placed ourselves in an ideal situation when we considered a plane wave as the incident packet. However, this difficulty is easily overcome if one considers {3}; in this formula the exponential represents the screening effect due to the charge of the electrons, and a is the radius of the “electron cloud”. Actually the screening effect should be determined with precise calculations, but in any case the exponential approximation is very good, and moreover the result does not change much if we choose other forms.

it: Sostituendo la formula per il potenziale abbiamo:

it: A questo punto abbiamo determinato la distribuzione di ampiezze
en: At this point we have determined the distribution of probability amplitudes; now we must interpret it and calculate the probability flux.

it: In origine avevamo un'onda piana che proveniva dalla direzione
en: Originally we had a plane wave coming from the direction {0}; the result, as figure {1} shows, is a plane wave going towards {2} plus a diverging spherical wave whose amplitude depends on the angle ϑ.

it: Nella realtà fisica non avremo un'onda piana infinitamente
en: In physical reality we will not have an infinitely extended plane wave but, as figure {0} shows, a narrow beam, very similar to a plane wave, but with a finite transverse size.

it: In queste condizioni solo l'onda sferica potrà arrivare
en: Under these conditions only the spherical wave can reach the detector, so it is only the spherical wave that we must consider to calculate the probability flux.

it: Ora passiamo al calcolo del flusso mediante la formula
en: Now we move on to the calculation of the flux by means of the formula {0}. The phase φ is the one found in the term {1} and is {2}{3}, so we have {4}.

it: Sostituendo nella formula per i vari termini abbiamo per il modulo
en: Substituting the various terms into the formula, we obtain for the modulus of the scattered flux-density vector:

it: Per la densità di flusso incidente dovuto all'onda
en: For the incident flux density due to the wave {0} we have:

it: La densità di flusso diffuso per una densità di flusso incidente unitaria
en: The scattered flux density for a unit incident flux density is given by the ratio:

it: Se moltiplichiamo questo termine per una superficie dS
en: If we multiply this term by a surface dS we obtain the flux through dS. If we want to calculate the flux associated with a solid angle dΩ we must consider the surface subtended by the angle dΩ, which is {0}. So the flux entering dΩ is:

it: Se sostituiamo
en: If we substitute {0} we obtain Rutherford's scattering formula:

it: Questa formula ci dà la densità di flusso diffuso per angolo solido, da un singolo
en: This formula gives us the scattered flux density per solid angle, from a single atom, when the incident flux has unit density.

it: Prima di tutto osserviamo che la formula di Rutherford è divergente
en: First of all we observe that Rutherford's formula diverges for {0}, so for small angles there can be no experimental agreement; we expected this because we made approximations valid only for fairly large angles.

it: Per quanto riguarda la dipendenza da
en: As for the dependence on {0}, figures 18 and 19 report the comparison between the values obtained experimentally and those calculated with the suitably scaled formula; figure 18 is on a linear scale, while figure 19 is on a logarithmic scale; the circles represent the measured points and the triangles represent the calculated points.

it: Come si vede c'è accordo per angoli maggiori di 10°.
en: As can be seen, there is agreement for angles greater than 10°.

it: Ora vedremo come si può determinare la carica nucleare
en: Now we will see how the nuclear charge can be determined by applying the complete Rutherford formula {0}.

it: Come abbiamo già detto questa formula ci dà la distribuzione
en: As we have already said, this formula gives us the distribution of the probability flux, scattered by a single atom and for a unit incident flux density. To apply it to our case we must first multiply it by the actual flux density, and then multiply it by the actual number of atoms that contribute to the scattering process:

it: Per ottenere il numero di atomi diffusori dobbiamo considerare
en: To obtain the number of scattering atoms we must consider the volume of gold struck by the beam and multiply it by n, the number of atoms per unit volume. The volume of gold struck by the beam can be calculated by multiplying the cross-section of the beam by the thickness s of the gold foil, so we have:

it: Il prodotto della densità del flusso per la sezione del fascio
en: The product of the flux density and the beam cross-section gives us the total incident flux, which we will denote by F, so we have:

it: Questa formula ci dà il flusso di probabilità per angolo solido. Se vogliamo
en: This formula gives us the probability flux per solid angle. If we want to calculate the flux of particles, we must consider that the passage of a particle is equivalent to the passage of a “unit of probability”, that is, when a unit of probability moves from one place to another it means that a particle has moved between these two places; so the formula that gives us the probability flux also gives us the flux of particles without needing to be corrected.

it: Se supponiamo che il detector sottende un angolo solido dΩ
en: If we assume that the detector subtends a solid angle dΩ, then we can calculate the number of particles per unit time that are detected:

it: Per poter applicare questa formula ci resta da determinare il flusso
en: To be able to apply this formula it remains to determine the total incident flux F. To determine this quantity we must compute the integral of the distribution obtained experimentally. The integral must be computed taking into account that the curve actually represents a surface, obtained by rotating the curve about the ordinate axis; so we do not have a simple integral, but a double integral. However, to obtain a rough estimate we will do the calculation based on the approximation shown in figure 20, that is, we will approximate the surface with a cylinder of base radius 10° and height equal to the maximum height of the surface, 27 particles per second in the solid angle dΩ.

en: So for the total flux we have:

en: For the total solid angle we have (Fig. {0}):

en: Substituting this value of F into the formula for the number of particles detected per unit time we have:

it: Per n, il numero di atomi per unità di volume, possiamo fare una stima
en: For n, the number of atoms per unit volume, we can make an estimate considering that an atom has a radius of about 1 Å ({0}), so it occupies a volume of about {1}; thus we have {2}.

it: Sostituendo nella formula i seguenti valori:
en: Substituting the following values into the formula:

it: A questo punto dobbiamo trovare la carica Q
en: At this point we must find the charge Q that makes this theoretical distribution coincide with the experimental distribution; for {0} we have the comparison shown in figure 22.

it: Quindi la nostra stima per la carica del nucleo dell'oro
en: So our estimate for the charge of the gold nucleus is {0}, corresponding to {1} electrons.

it: Misure più precise portano ad una carica pari a 79 volte
en: More precise measurements lead to a charge equal to 79 times that of the electron.

it: Noi non abbiamo voluto eseguire una misura molto precisa
en: We did not want to carry out a very precise measurement; our aim was only to show how it is possible to determine the nuclear charge and the number of electrons contained in an atom.
